mcq.question_md: Every day your program runs `new FileWriter("attendance.csv")` and writes one line. After a week, the file contains only **today's** line. Why?
options: The file system limits each file to one line per program
FileWriter without the append flag overwrites the file each time it opens
You must call flush() once per line or earlier lines are dropped
CSV files can only be extended using the Files class
mcq.explanation_md: `new FileWriter("attendance.csv")` truncates the file to empty the moment it opens — so each run erases the previous six days. To add to the end, pass the append flag: `new FileWriter("attendance.csv", true)`. The tempting wrong answer is `flush()`: forgetting to flush/close can lose the *current* run's unwritten bytes, but it doesn't erase data already saved on disk — the overwrite-on-open behaviour does that. This one mistake has eaten more beginner log files than any other line of Java.
